content_english:
I have to admit, this VF model looks much better than the previous ones, even more elegant than the more expensive VF7 and VF8.

The downside is that there’s no dedicated A/C control button — you have to use the touchscreen. Personally, I really dislike this because it’s inconvenient and even dangerous if you want to adjust the A/C while driving. Plus, VF’s screen sometimes freezes and becomes unresponsive. Aside from that, everything else seems decent based on my first impression from Tipcar’s video. I think VF doesn’t need to put such a powerful motor on a service-oriented vehicle. 201 hp and 280 Nm is way overkill for a B+ MPV segment. Around 150 hp and 180 Nm would be more than enough. That would save more battery —> longer range, reduce production costs, and VF could use that to add dedicated A/C knobs or leather seat upholstery.

The video isn't wrong. The VF8 and VF9 don't have an interior switch to adjust the headlight height. What you're calling "auto-leveling" is actually the LED Matrix. It's a beam pattern that changes based on different oncoming light conditions—it's not headlight height adjustment.

content_original:
Kính g?i: VIETNAM ROAD TRIP
Theo góp ý c?a VNRT v? vi?c chia s? innova cross máy x?ng V2.0 2024, thì tôi có ?ánh giá nh?
category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Styling / Design; Value Perception
parameter: Ride Quality; Seat Comfort; Cabin Space; Hill Climbing Ability; Exterior Design; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
To: VIETNAM ROAD TRIP
Following VNRT's suggestion about sharing the Innova Cross gasoline V2.0 2024, I have the following review:
I have previously bought and driven the Innova 2013, Mazda CX-5 2017, Honda CR-V 2019, Fortuner 2021, and now I'm driving this Innova. Honestly, the cars I've driven before had quite a few pros and cons — some were comfortable and drove smoothly but cramped with suffocating interior space, others were bouncy. But when I got behind the wheel of this Innova Cross 2024 gasoline V2.0, after driving to Da Lat through mountain passes and small alleys with 45-degree slopes, and also experiencing a trip to Ca Mau and back, I haven't found any drawbacks with this car. All 7 friends I brought along praised it for its smooth ride and very comfortable seating. As for me, I drove as the 8th person for a few hundred kilometers and felt less tired than with the previous cars I bought. There are many advantages in detailed reviews, but there are too many to list — let others experience and review them. On the way, when stopping for meals and at rest stops, quite a few people came and asked me, "Is this the new Innova, sir?" I don't know why they asked so much... probably it's hot. Thank you VNRT very much. Wishing you health and success!

Kính g?i: VIETNAM ROAD TRIP
Theo góp ý c?a VNRT v? vi?c chia s? innova cross máy x?ng V2.0 2024, thì tôi có ?ánh giá nh?
category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Ride Quality; Seat Comfort; Cabin Space; Engine Power
content_english:
Dear VIETNAM ROAD TRIP,

Following VNRT's request to share about the Innova Cross gasoline V2.0 2024, I have the following review:

I have previously bought and driven various models: Innova 2013, Mazda CX-5 2017, Honda CR-V 2019, Fortuner 2021, and currently I am driving this Innova. To be honest, the cars I drove had many pros and cons (some were comfortable and smooth but cramped, interior space felt stuffy; some were stable...). But when I drove this Innova Cross 2024 gasoline V2.0, after going to Da Lat through mountain passes, steep 45-degree alleys, and also experiencing a trip to Ca Mau and back, I haven't found any drawbacks. All 7 of my friends who came along praised this car for its smooth ride and very comfortable seating. As for me, the driver being the 8th person in the car, after driving a few hundred kilometers, I felt less tired than with the previous cars I bought. There are many detailed advantages, but too many to list— let others experience and evaluate. While stopping for meals and at rest stops along the way, quite a few people came to ask me, "Is this the new Innova, sir?" I don't know why they asked so much... probably it's hot right now.

Thank you VNRT very much. Wishing you health and success!

content_english: I came back to watch this clip when I heard that the Limo Green surpassed the X-Pander. The steering wheel, I see it's been cut down and it's a bit ugly (already ugly from VinFast). Actually, I don't use the steering wheel much, except for voice commands, cruise control, and adjusting the volume. But since I go with family, my wife handles the volume control and voice commands. Everything else is perfectly fine—even without counting the free charging for two years, it's great value. Because the service is designed to be most comfortable for customers, and if the plastic parts wear out, they're easy to replace at low cost. The decision to cut into the luggage compartment is a very smart move by the manufacturer. When the car is fully booked, 90% of the time it's office workers. If you book the car to carry luggage to the airport, you definitely won't go with eight people, so the third row ends up with plenty of empty space. But if eight people with luggage are going to the airport, you'd have to book a 16-seater. I feel proud of Vietnam for having such a quality car and understanding the drivers. Respect to Mr. Vuong!

content_english:
I’ve been using the Indo-imported Veloz, took delivery in June 2022, so it’s been 3.5 years. Here’s my impression:
- The car is solid; the engine and interior/exterior haven’t degraded (inevitable wear and tear over time is unavoidable).
- Stable, even over rough roads. Can’t deny that since the car is heavy, it rides stable and composed. It doesn’t have cruise control, so driving from HCMC to Da Lat is a bit tiring.
- Practical. The car has plenty of cubbies, comfortable sofa-like seats, and the folded third row gives very generous space, suitable for a family.
- The engine is smooth, not aggressive. With 4 people on mixed roads I get 13 km/l, on highways about 14.x km/l. Never reached 15 km/l.

Kính g?i: VIETNAM ROAD TRIP
Theo góp ý c?a VNRT v? vi?c chia s? innova cross máy x?ng V2.0 2024, thì tôi có ?ánh giá nh?
category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space
parameter: Ride Quality; Seat Comfort; Cabin Space; Exterior Design; Terrain Capability; Long Distance Comfort
content_english:
Dear VIETNAM ROAD TRIP,

Following VNRT’s suggestion to share about the Innova Cross gasoline V2.0 2024, I have the following review:

I have previously owned and driven various cars: Innova 2013, Mazda CX-5 2017, Honda CRV 2019, Fortuner 2021, and now I’m driving this Innova. Honestly, the cars I’ve driven all had many pros and cons (one was comfortable and smooth but cramped, with tight interior space; another was bouncy...). But when I got behind the wheel of this Innova Cross 2024 gasoline V2.0, after driving to Da Lat through winding mountain passes, narrow alleys with 45-degree slopes, and also going to Ca Mau and back, I haven’t found any drawbacks to this car. All 7 of my friends who rode with me praised the car for being smooth and very comfortable. As for me, as the 8th person driving the entire journey of a few hundred kilometers, I felt more comfortable than in the previous cars I owned. There are many detailed reviews with plenty of advantages, but there are too many to list, so I’ll leave it for others to experience and review. Along the way, when stopping for meals and at rest points, many people came up and asked me, “Is this the new Innova, brother?” I don’t know why they asked so much... maybe it’s hot right now.

Thank you VNRT very much. Wishing you health and success!

category: Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception; Brand Perception; Value Perception
parameter: Platform Architecture; Engine Options; Competitor Position; Product Strategy; Sales Performance
content_english:
Looking at the 2024 Innova now, it only just now switched to the TNGA platform and front-wheel drive, making you realize Toyota has been resting on its laurels for far too long.

The previous-generation Innova had a rear-wheel drive IMV platform and even offered 2.5, 2.4, and 2.8 diesel engines, but Toyota Vietnam refused to equip them like in Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, and India, simply out of fear of "stepping on" the Hilux and Fortuner. That was a completely wrong mindset, which is why the Xpander overtook it — no injustice there. Even the fact that the 2021 facelift Innova in the Philippines removed the 2.0 petrol engine due to poor sales and kept only the 2.8 diesel is clear enough evidence.

parameter: Platform Update; Diesel Engine Options; Competitor Comparison; Engine Availability; Brand Strategy
content_english:
Looking at the new 2024 Innova now that it finally switched to the TNGA platform and front-wheel drive, you can really see that Toyota has been resting on its laurels for way too long.

The previous-generation Innova with its IMV platform and rear-wheel drive even had 2.5, 2.4, and 2.8 diesel engines, but Toyota Vietnam refused to offer them like in Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, and India — all because they were afraid of "stepping on" the Hilux and Fortuner. A completely wrong mentality that led to the Xpander overtaking it, and honestly, it deserved to lose its crown. Even the fact that the 2021 facelift Innova in the Philippines dropped the 2.0 petrol engine due to poor sales and kept only the 2.8 diesel is clear proof of that.

content_english: You haven't understood the operating principle of e-Power. It uses the battery at low speeds, uses the generator at high speeds, and above 80 km/h it uses both battery and generator. When driving, it normally uses the battery; when the battery drops below 30%, it runs on gasoline, simultaneously recharging the battery and supplying the electric motor. When the battery reaches 90%, it stops charging and runs entirely on the generator to let the battery cool down and stabilize before using it again. As I said, it decides whether to use the battery or generator based on speed; at high speeds it uses both.

content_english: @linhngo7047 You explained correctly. When driving in the city, the regenerative braking system works continuously (every time we release the gas pedal or brake) it operates. Conversely, in the city, you drive slowly, so you need less electricity, thus it saves a lot. But when driving on the highway, when we double the speed, the electricity consumption is not double but nearly triple (on average, when we increase speed by 10%, wind resistance increases by a bit over 20% (approximate calculation)...). Meanwhile, on the highway, the regenerative braking system works very little (according to calculations, only about 17-20% of what it does in the city). Therefore, driving on the highway consumes much more fuel. If I may: the regenerative braking system is the component that converts excess kinetic energy into electricity and charges the battery.

Lý do epower ch?y trong ph? tiêt ki?m h?n cao t?c là vì:
-Nguyên lí nó v?n d? là xe thu?n ?i?n ?c g?n máy phát ?i?n nên
content_english:
The reason ePower is more fuel-efficient in the city than on the highway is:
- Its principle is basically an electric car with a generator attached, so the car operates just like a pure EV.
- Unlike a gasoline car, an EV is more energy-efficient at low speeds than at high speeds.
- When driving in the city at an average speed of 40 km/h, the battery discharges less current to the motor, so it consumes less battery >>> the generator has to work less to recharge the battery.
- When driving on the highway at speeds over 60 km/h, the current draw is high >>> the generator operates at high power to increase the charging current to the battery.

I can't understand how e-Power works. The gasoline engine in the car is definitely just a generator, there's no other possibility. It supplies power to the battery, agreed on that.

So why does it consume three times more fuel on the highway than in the city?

You can't say that on the highway the engine runs 100% of the time because the electric motor gets energy from the battery, not from the engine. Unless the e-Power battery is being charged and discharged at the same time? Then the battery would drop to 80% in the first year, not after 5 years.

Or the car has a mechanism that allows the engine to directly power the electric motor without going through the battery — that's the only logical explanation I can come up with for such high highway consumption. But if that's the case, the name "e-Power" is a total waste, because if the engine only charged the battery, fuel consumption would definitely be much lower, about the same as city driving.

content_original: Nguyên lý ho?t ??ng c?a e-power là: ??ng c? x?ng ch?y máy phát ?i?n, máy phát ?i?n truy?n ?i?n cho motor kéo xe, ??ng th
category: Powertrain / Performance; New Tech; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Energy Consumption; e-Power System; Acceleration; Engine Power
content_english:
The working principle of e-power is: the gasoline engine runs the generator, the generator supplies electricity to the electric motor that drives the car, while also charging the battery if its reserve drops below 50%.

When accelerating, the motor needs more energy; the additional energy is drawn from the battery, so the gasoline engine doesn't need to increase its output, which helps save fuel.

When driving in the city, the car only needs low energy. Therefore, the ECU instructs the gasoline engine to operate only in an optimized, fuel-efficient mode, resulting in very impressive consumption (2.7L/100km).

When the car runs on the highway or climbs long slopes, the gasoline engine must operate at its full designed output. At that point, the engine is no longer very fuel-efficient.

Note that the Nissan Kicks' gasoline engine is somewhat small, so when driving on slopes or highways, it feels a bit underpowered.

content_original: Có gì mà khó hi?u. Ví d? ??ng c? nissan kick luôn ho?t ??ng ? 1 m?c rpm ?n ??nh, ko thay ??i, t?o ra 1 côg su?t ??nh m?c
category: Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception
parameter: Energy Consumption; Acceleration; Regenerative Braking; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
What's so hard to understand? For example, the Nissan Kicks' engine always runs at a stable, fixed RPM, generating a constant rated power output of 100%. When driving slowly or stopping—two common situations—the car doesn't need the full 100% power, only about 60%, for instance. So the engine sends 60% to the electric motor, and the remaining 40% is stored in the battery.

When the battery is full, the engine shuts off completely, and the battery continues to power the electric motor. The engine doesn't run all the time, so it doesn't waste fuel. That's reason number 1.

Reason number 2: the regenerative braking system. It recovers energy when braking and stores it in the battery. For example, heading down an infinitely long slope, braking all the way would be more than enough to fully charge the battery.

Reason number 3: the conversion efficiency from thermal energy to electrical energy in the gasoline engine, and from electrical energy to kinetic energy in the Kicks' electric motor, is higher than the thermal-to-kinetic energy transfer through a conventional combustion drivetrain. Also, the Kicks, like a pure battery electric vehicle, delivers high torque from the moment you start moving (thanks to the electric motor), maximizing energy efficiency.

Reason number 4: the vehicle's weight is lighter than both conventional gasoline cars and pure EVs. The Kicks has a gasoline engine, a generator, an electric motor, and a small battery pack. Traditional gasoline cars have an engine, transmission, and complex drivetrain. EVs have a heavy battery pack and an electric motor. Generally, both of those types are heavier, so they consume more fuel/energy.

Reason number 5: not a single bit of surplus energy is wasted. Even when idling with the AC on while parked, the car doesn't waste energy. For instance, if the AC uses only 20% of the engine's output, the remaining 80% goes into the battery. When the car runs, that stored electricity is used without needing the engine to run. So 100% of the generated power is fully utilized.

All things considered, the Kicks is actually a 100% electric car—it's not a hybrid. Because the definition of a hybrid is: "A hybrid vehicle, often called a hybrid or electric hybrid, is a vehicle that uses two power sources: an internal combustion engine and an electric motor. Its operation combines the internal combustion engine and the electric motor for optimal performance." That means the car must be directly driven by the internal combustion engine (through a transmission) and the electric motor. But in the Kicks, the gasoline engine does not directly drive the wheels. So fundamentally, the Kicks is like an electric car that just happens to carry a backup generator.

content_english:
Allow me to reply, sir!  

We have 3 main reasons:  

1/ The electric motor can operate just enough for the vehicle's driving needs without causing as much energy loss as a gasoline engine (as mentioned in the clip above), so in urban environments, the amount of electrical energy needed is very small.  

2/ - The gasoline engine is designed to always generate excess electricity in every situation and driving condition of the vehicle.  
   - The e-Power engine mechanism: the gasoline engine produces electricity, which is then transmitted directly to the inverter (power management unit), then to the electric motor, and the excess energy is sent to the battery for storage. When the battery is full, the gasoline engine will stop working entirely to use the stored electricity from the battery.  

-> From reasons (1) and the two points above, it can be seen that in the city, the excess energy from the gasoline engine's operation will be greater than in other environments -> the battery gets charged faster, and the gasoline engine gets more rest.  

3/ The e-Pedal Step technology on the vehicle helps regenerate more energy in urban environments, optimizing energy savings.  

It can be seen that under fuel-saving conditions, the battery will work occasionally. But overall, it will still avoid the situation of continuous charging and discharging that leads to reduced battery life. You can rest assured.  

This is what I know and was clearly trained by the manufacturer. If I'm wrong or there's anything missing, please help supplement, everyone ^^

category: Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance
content_english: A generator: that is, higher efficiency than conventional direct-drive cars. When you accelerate and shift gears, the mechanical efficiency is said to be around 33%, so there's always waste and excess energy lost. Roughly calculated, that's still 8L/100km, and many city-driven cars go up to 12L/100km. Second: the hotter the engine, the more waste and the lower the efficiency. But with this electric car, the generator engine achieves a higher conversion efficiency of mechanical and thermal energy—let's say 44%. Roughly calculated, it's already several times more economical than a regular car, my friend. When running on the highway, the gasoline engine doesn't have to work at full capacity; it only produces a moderate amount of power, just enough to supply the electric motor moderately, and the surplus charges the battery. If you floor the accelerator to accelerate, the inverter will take the missing electricity from the battery to compensate, instead of forcing the gas engine to run—hence extremely high efficiency. Efficiency and performance in this car are optimized without waste, giving a convincingly incredible figure. As for conventional cars, the waste is expelled through the exhaust pipe, you know.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception; New Tech
parameter: Energy Consumption; Maintenance Cost; Value for Money; Competitor Comparison; Battery Technology
content_english:
Comparing Corolla Cross V and HV (same equipment, only difference is the engine, price gap 95 million VND):
1. Using gasoline price: 23,500 VND/liter
   V version consumes 9 liters
   HV version consumes 4.8 liters
   So per 100km, the HV saves 4.2 liters = 98,700 VND
   Price difference of 95 million / 98,700 = 96,251 km before the two cars' total costs equal. This shows that the HV is not cheaper than the V until you've driven 96,251 km. If you average 20,000 km per year, that's about 5 years.
2. When it comes to warranty, maintenance, and battery replacement (for the HV), the HV is definitely more expensive than the V.
   In short, the HV costs more to own. It does not save the user money.
3. The Kicks is 100 million cheaper than the Corolla Cross HV, so I think if you want to save money, buy the Kicks. As for whether it's good or not, come back in 5 years and decide. The battery technology in cars is at a much higher level now; it's not like phone batteries.

content_original: v? b?n ch?t thì xe Hybrid và E-Power ??u có ?i?m chung là kh?i ??ng và ch?y v?n t?c th?p b?ng n?ng l??ng pin (t? s?c), n
category: Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance
parameter: Power and Smoothness; City Fuel Economy; Highway Fuel Economy; Heavy Load Fuel Economy
content_english: In essence, both Hybrid and e-Power cars share the common feature of starting and running at low speeds using battery power (from charging). The difference lies at high speeds, where one switches to a conventional gasoline car while the other still uses an electric motor but powered by a gasoline engine. In terms of power and smoothness, e-Power is likely superior to Hybrid (specifically the Corolla Cross). As for fuel efficiency, if you're driving in the city, the Nissan Kicks clearly wins because the gasoline engine barely works. For slow, short-distance trips, the Kicks is essentially a pure electric car, and the gasoline engine is just there for show. In contrast, with the Corolla Cross, the electric motor only assists with starting and low-speed driving, after which the gasoline engine takes over to drive the wheels. As for highway or long-distance driving, to be honest, I still trust Hybrid more, because it essentially becomes a traditional gasoline car at high speeds—just fill the tank and go. Meanwhile, the e-Power car runs on battery at low speeds, and when going a bit faster, the gasoline engine charges the battery, which then powers the electric motor. On the highway, it directly transfers power from the gasoline engine to the electric motor, with the battery serving as backup only for lower speeds. Under heavy loads, it will certainly consume more fuel than a Hybrid (even with battery assistance).

category: Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Brand Perception; Other
parameter: NVH; Energy Consumption; After Sales Service; Durability and Safety
content_english:
I bought a Veloz Cross Top in March 2024 at Toyota Ha Dong and have had over a month of experience using it. My assessment of this car is as follows:

1. The car has a fairly high level of noise from the floor, the four wheel arches, and engine bay echo compared to the Xpander. I upgraded the sound insulation with Russian materials for 12 million VND for the entire floor and body. As a result, the feeling when driving over potholes or on the highway has improved significantly.

2. Fuel consumption is a point to note, with 4.8 liters/100km on the highway when Eco mode is activated and 6 liters/100km in the city.

3. I want to emphasize Toyota's after-sales service, with friendly reception, spacious waiting area, and provision of drinks and coffee for customers. I can clearly feel the improvement in customer care compared to previous years, probably because the golden days are over so Toyota is fighting to regain market share.

4. Finally, the durability and safety of the car are important factors for me when choosing to buy a car. This mid-range car meets those requirements.

5. I want to emphasize that my review is fair and not intended to criticize any car brand. I bought the car to serve my beloved family, and these reviews may help others make better decisions when buying a car.

* Note that upgrading the car's sound insulation may be an investment worth considering.

category: Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Brand Perception; Safety & ADAS
parameter: NVH; Energy Consumption; After Sales Service; Durability and Safety
content_english:
I bought a Veloz Cross Top car in March 2024 at Toyota Ha Dong and have had over a month of usage experience. My evaluation of this car is as follows:

1. The car has quite high noise levels from the floor and the four wheel wells, along with echo from the engine compartment compared to the Xpander. I upgraded the soundproofing system with Russian materials, a package worth 12 million VND for the entire floor and body. As a result, the feel when driving on bumpy gravel roads or highways has improved significantly.

2. Fuel consumption is a point to note, with consumption of 4.8 liters/100km on highways when ECO mode is activated and 6 liters/100km in urban areas.

3. I want to emphasize Toyota's after-sales service, with friendly reception, spacious environment, and providing drinks and coffee to customers. I feel a clear improvement in customer care compared to previous years, probably because the golden age has passed so Toyota is fighting to regain market share.

4. Finally, the durability and safety of the car are important factors for me when choosing to buy a car. This mid-range car meets that requirement.

5. I want to emphasize that my review is fair and not intended to criticize any car brand. I bought the car with my money to serve my beloved family, and these reviews can help others make better decisions when buying a car.

* Note that upgrading soundproofing for the car can be a worthwhile investment to consider.

content_english:
I just drove my 2022 model from Binh Duong to the western region. Ride comfort is average; on slightly rough roads or village and commune paths, there’s a lot of chassis vibration, but on highways or city roads, the ride is smooth and the steering feels refined. This car is better suited for regular roads. If you often drive on country roads, you should pick a car with a body‑on‑frame chassis instead.

Fuel consumption is very good.

As for features and interior, they’re hard to fault at this price point. Overall, it’s quite decent.

In terms of spaciousness and comfort when carrying many people, the Xpander is the undisputed king.

All in all, for this price range, I’d choose it. If you have more budget, the Carnival is better, and if you have even more, the VW Viloran.

category: Other; Other; Powertrain / Performance; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Value Perception
parameter: Build Quality; Underbody Protection; Driving Dynamics; ADAS Features; Air Conditioning; NVH; NVH; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
I am a Veloz Top user. I idolize Tipcar, and thanks to Tipcar's reviews, I have a lot of trust. Right now there are too many posts criticizing the Veloz for its grille and rough ride. Let me share:  
- Grille: True. But it's not related to vehicle safety, because the ability to absorb impact forces is good.  
- Underbody skid plate: I ordered an additional skid plate from the dealer, it feels very solid when the dealer imported it. Proves Toyota truly respects customer feedback.  
- Driving experience: I drive on the highway, using S mode + Power, oh my god, it's awesome, feels like a race car. Of course, the driving feel can't compare to cars over 1 billion VND. I drive to serve my family, not for Formula 1 racing.  
- TSS: Safe, supportive, helps me in many situations. I have a small child, so TSS really convinced me. When driving long, the car even reminds me "Take a break and drink coffee." I'm extremely satisfied. TSS has performed its job excellently.  
- Air conditioning: Freezing cold, seriously.  
- Sound insulation: Still okay on the highway. Nothing noisy at all. I'm satisfied.  
- Driving in the rain: Normal, I don't hear any tin roof noise, folks. You critics are weird.  
I also love the Xpander, but I chose the Veloz because it suits my personality, style, work, and the TSS safety system will stay with me for the next 5-10 years.  
Finally, I truly appreciate Tipcar for helping me make the right choice.  
And I am satisfied because Safety comes first.  
If you want a perfect car, then buy a supercar.  
No car is without its pros and cons.  
Best regards. Just a little opinion.

INNOVA 2007-2015 automatic version

Advantages
1: Spacious interior
2: Very good sound insulation
3: Uses a separate ladder frame
4: Very high durability
5: Excellent resale value
6: Brand from Japan
7: Nationwide dealer network
8: Rear-wheel drive

1: Acceleration from 0-100 km/h takes up to 14.5 seconds

3: 2.0 engine with 134 hp & 183 Nm is a bit weak when fully loaded on mountain roads and highways

5: Hydraulic power steering is heavier than the new 2016-2022 generation
6: Fuel consumption ranges from 9-10 L/100 km
7: There is a droning noise (a bit uncomfortable on long trips)

content_english: Regarding interior materials, I don't fully agree with you. In this segment, I only highly rate the Honda BRV for its interior materials. The rest (except the Stargazer X, which I haven't seen) are all equally bad. They feel even worse than the plastic on the old Vios J that I bought back after 8 years of taxi service. The seats from Mitsu are softer, while the others feel like sitting on cheap gaming chairs. If a rear passenger reaches for something in the seatback pocket, the front passenger can clearly feel it. The ergonomics of these "chicken-feet" MPVs are even worse than the VF5 (from the driver's position). In short, don't expect much from the interior in this segment. The best parts are the air conditioning and spaciousness; otherwise, they lose to B-segment sedans. I've come to this conclusion because I'm currently looking at 7-seater cars for my family. I was thinking of saving money compared to the Everest, but it looks like I won't be able to buy anything other than a Ford.

Veloz review

- Many options and technology

category: Comfort & Space; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Other; Other; Safety & ADAS; New Tech; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception; Other; Value Perception
parameter: Suspension; Collision Avoidance; NVH; Cabin Smell; Door Closing Sound; ADAS Features; Infotainment Display; Air Conditioning; Seat Comfort; Acceleration; Maintenance Cost; Operating Cost; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
I’ve been using the Veloz for 3 years now, I was among the first to order it.  
- Suspension: On gravel roads, it gives a very real feel, the sound is quite clear. At first it was a bit annoying, but later on, it became an advantage for me to truly feel the road conditions.  
- Safety system: The car automatically brakes before an actual collision. It saved me from a rear-end crash when the car in front suddenly slammed on its brakes at a traffic light.  
- Highway: At almost 120 km/h, it’s smooth and quiet. Even my wife, kids, and elderly parents found it comfortable and pleasantly surprising.  
- Car smell: The car completely convinced me because it doesn’t have any unpleasant odor. APPROVED.  
- Body panels: Honestly, I bought the Veloz because of this channel’s review. Other people kept complaining about the thin panels, the paint, etc.—it sounded scary. But with a 5-star ANCAP rating, once I read the technical explanations, I realized thin or thick each has its own advantages. The key is that the doors close with a solid thud. I was hesitant at first because of all the criticism, but after I bought it, I saw for myself—the doors close with a satisfying, even refined sound. Just gently push the door and it closes smoothly, like a polite exchange between two sides. APPROVED. This is the right mindset for driving a car.  
- The car has saved me from many dangerous situations. Lane departure warnings, collision alerts, and sudden overtaking by other cars all produce clear warning sounds. So I consider the car a family member.  
- Even on my wedding anniversary, the car screen displayed "Congratulations" to me—I don’t know how, but I was really happy. Drive for a while, and the car suggests taking a coffee break. So cute.  
- I’m not just saying this for no reason.  
But with the Veloz, apart from high parking costs in the city, every other expense is low. And my car hasn’t had any breakdowns in 3 years. The only issue is I scratched it myself once. That’s the truth, guys.  
The AC system is excellent, the seats are okay, and even over speed bumps it’s smooth.  
- On mountain roads, turn on Sport mode, use paddle shifters, it runs smoothly and overtakes like a beast.  
- I think the new Mitsubishi Destinator looks great too, guys, something to consider.  
Overall, I’ve been using the Veloz and I’m SATISFIED FOR 3 YEARS. PERIOD.  
WHEN THE CAR REACHES THE END OF ITS LIFE AND I HAVE TO SWITCH TO AN EV, I’LL SWAP.  
VELOZ 7-SEATER, MY FAMILY IS HAPPY BECAUSE IT KEEPS US SAFE.  
TO THOSE WHO KEEP CRITICIZING THE THIN PANELS, PLEASE STOP. BREAK THAT UNHEALTHY HABIT.  
SCIENTISTS HAVE ALREADY MEASURED EVERYTHING.  
WISHES TO EVERYONE FOR CHOOSING YOUR DREAM CAR!

content_english: I drive a Veloz 4V, and personally I think it's suitable for family use, city driving, and good roads for trips and camping on paved roads. At its price point, I find it practical for the features and safety it offers. All things considered, the Veloz and Xpander are relatively difficult to drive because they're big cars—the suspension, steering system, and engine power can't satisfy every aspect, so you really need to understand the car, modulate the throttle, brake, and steering—none of it can be rushed. Fuel economy, safety, easy to buy and easy to resell—that's still very Toyota. Personally, I chose the Veloz for the sofa-like seats. Even though I bought the top trim, I still added a 360 camera, replaced the lights, added sensors and cruise control—these cost a bit but are simple to do, warranty and maintenance are normal, and most importantly, I like it :))))))

My goal was long-term family use, mainly for leisure—about 5,000 km per year—so I didn’t care much about parts replacement or resale value. In the end I was torn between the Xpender and the Stargazer because I was buying it for leisure use, just about 5,000 km a year. I finally chose the Stargazer X (2024), even though I still had a soft spot for the Xpender. If you compare buying the Xpender, it turns out you have tens of millions left over—for what? I already have enough, and I can go out all year and not use it all up. I advise those with a flexible budget who are buying for family use to get the Xpender because it’s the most balanced of the three, which is why it’s the top seller. If your budget is tight and you don’t want to make installment payments, the Stargazer is the best choice (just pay as you go). Even if you spend tens of millions on ride-hailing, you’ll still struggle all year, folks. In the end, a car is just a shelter from rain and sun; the safest factor is still the driver. Everyone please help me with advice: for a budget of around 800 million VND (with tire upgrade), what good options are there for driving in Hanoi mostly, occasionally on the highway? High ground clearance is good.

The Veloz has many options and the Toyota Safety Sense package I'm really interested in, and it's within my budget. But I asked two Toyota dealers and both had the same situation: if I buy additional accessories (~40 million) or pay an extra premium of about 30 million, I can get the car delivered immediately in April-May; otherwise, I'd have to queue and wait another 2-3 months. Plus the fabric seats... So I'm fed up and don't want to buy the Veloz anymore.

By the way, Tipcar is the most objective, detailed, and dedicated car review channel I've ever seen. Thank you to Viet Anh and the team.

Abroad, cars last 5 to 10 years. In Vietnam, they last over 20 years. The AT transmission has proven itself for over 20 years without breaking down. Stargazer và Veloz dù t
category: Safety & ADAS; Safety & ADAS; Safety & ADAS; Safety & ADAS
parameter: Occupant Protection; NCAP Rating; Chassis Strength; ADAS Features
content_english:
Regarding NCAP, I have the following feedback: The star rating does not reflect the actual quality of the car's chassis. The Stargazer and Veloz, despite being loaded with equipment, have very low occupant protection scores. Here I'm referring to protection points, not the number of stars. The Xpander only got 4 stars because it lacked active safety features like ADAS, while its occupant protection score was nearly maxed out—even though the test car was a 2018 model with only 2 airbags—scoring 39.08 points for adult front occupants and 18.09 for child rear occupants, much higher than many other CUVs/SUVs. => Best chassis.

Meanwhile, the Veloz and Stargazer scored extremely low, the lowest in their segment, even worse than the XL7 with only 2 airbags. But the VELOZ got a boost because the test car had ADAS (active safety), allowing it to achieve 5 stars, yet its protection score was only 34.88 points for adult front occupants and 17.17 for child rear occupants, despite having 6 airbags. The Stargazer is the worst—despite being fully loaded with 6 airbags, its protection scores were just 31.21 for adult front occupants and 14.48 for child rear occupants, and even with ADAS pulling it up, it only got 4 stars => Worst in the segment. That's also why users complain that the Veloz feels flimsy, and the Stargazer too. Just because a car gets 5 NCAP stars doesn't mean it's good.

The Xpander now comes standard with 6 airbags, not 2. And ADAS is most important at high speeds—it's like the driver's second pair of eyes—because once you're going over 60 km/h, ADAS is more of a luxury than a necessity. But when driving at 30-40-50 km/h, ADAS just becomes annoying because the driver can easily react on their own.

content_english: The Xpander accelerates slower than the Veloz because it's over 100kg heavier. The sound insulation, whether at low or high speeds, is noticeably better than the Veloz. The armrest is larger than the Veloz's. The driver's seat doesn't have foot space issues like the Veloz does. The Xpander's lighting system completely outshines the Veloz's. When accelerating, the Xpander's engine doesn't roar like the Veloz. When climbing a hill, just step on the gas and you'll feel the difference. The AT transmission is durable enough that you never have to worry about it (it's just that the production cost is high, so manufacturers don't want to use it). The Xpander has been out for many years now, so its drawbacks have been improved, making the driving experience better than the Veloz. As for parts, the Veloz's are very expensive—a pair of mirrors at the dealer sells for nearly 20 million VND; since it's a new model, where would you find cheap parts? Just look at the sales figures: consumers choose to buy, and you'll know right away which car has the advantage. The Veloz will definitely need a major upgrade next time before it can be perfected.

content_english: Basically, back in the day we didn't have many choices. I bought a motorcycle, and there weren't many options: Chinese bikes were terrible, Korean ones weren't available, and Japanese only had Honda, Yamaha, Suzuki (Honda was fuel-efficient and had good design. Yamaha had a powerful engine but rusted quickly. Suzuki was cheap in every way, at least the price was low). As for cars: almost no one dared to buy Chinese ones. Korean cars rusted quickly. Japanese cars had good prices, were fuel-efficient, durable, and rusted less. Now we also have VinFast: fuel-efficient, design okay, durability okay (I think VinFast does everything well), many service centers, but resale value drops fast (this is both an advantage and a disadvantage—advantage is the price is accessible, disadvantage is low resale value). Electric cars are a bit inconvenient for charging, but one charge lasts a week (so actually not that inconvenient). The price of electric cars compared to their value is quite cheap: because the frame of an electric car is 2–3 times heavier than a gasoline car, so the material costs must be significantly higher, not to mention safety standards, and so on.

content_english:
So far, the competition between Toyota and Mitsubishi has been as follows:
Zace vs. Jolie (Jolie was a bit more popular). Toyota immediately launched the Innova, and Jolie faded into history.
Lancer vs. Camry, Corolla (at first they competed evenly). Later, Toyota upgraded the Camry and Corolla and launched the Vios, and then Lancer also faded into history.
Pajero vs. Land Cruiser (at first Pajero seemed to overshadow Land Cruiser), but later Toyota upgraded, and the Land Cruiser became a symbol of successful people, while Pajero dropped to a mainstream level.
Grandis vs. Previa (initially, the Grandis was an icon of automatic transmission cars. Toyota launched the Previa, and Grandis sales were disastrous, leading to its discontinuation. Meanwhile, the Previa continued with several improved versions.
Outlander vs. Innova (Outlander never made it onto the best-selling car list like the Innova, which dominated for a long time).
Expander vs. Rush, Avanza (the Expander took the crown), but now that Toyota has improved and launched the new Veloz and Avanza, who knows if the Expander's fate will be the same as its Mitsubishi predecessors?

content_english: Hmm, the Stargazer wants to compete with the Xpander and Veloz? Then it needs to upgrade, but the key is that the price must stay the same as the 2025 version to have a real chance. If they just upgrade a few details but raise the price, it's worthless in terms of competition. It's already at the bottom of the segment, and if it wants to sit at the same table, it has to upgrade without changing the price—only then is there any real hope. If they tweak a few things and then jack up the price, it's pointless. It will be left behind forever. Over and over, people on forums talk about all these fancy terms like "breakthrough strategy" and so on, but the truth is that's the strategy for manufacturers and dealers—users rarely care about all that bluster, because their need is a product that optimizes quality relative to real value.

category: Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Safety & ADAS; New Tech; Safety & ADAS; New Tech; Other; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Brand Perception
parameter: Cabin Space; Engine Power; Transmission; ADAS Features; Parking Camera; Braking System; Infotainment Display; Horn; Lighting; NVH; Engine Noise; Road Noise; Wind Noise; Ride Quality; After Sales Service
content_english:
I mainly drive in the city, 90% of the time is for taking my kids to school, I chose the Veloz Top because:
- Widest, can comfortably fit 7 plump people, if only for the family it's still ok
- The second-row seats are like a sofa. I fold down the front passenger seat and let the kids sit in a child seat in the second row; they have a wide view (seeing through 5 big windows, 2 side windows, the passenger window, and the windshield), they can also watch videos from the entertainment screen (with an Android box) or mount an iPad on the dashboard. The front passenger seat when folded down can be a storage area for the kids (backpacks, snacks, other items), and when they need something they can reach it themselves or I can turn around and hand it to them (no seat obstruction). This is a convenience point that any parents in a similar situation can consider, because almost all the time using the car is for carrying kids and family.
- Engine is adequate for city use, though the D-CVT gearbox takes some getting used to
- Safety features are very suitable for city driving: collision warning (city traffic often jams), front vehicle departure alert (sometimes forget when interacting with kids), blind spot warning with sound (very necessary in the city; when signaling to change lanes, I feel confident; many times I just signal and if there's no beep, I change lanes without looking at the mirror; if there's a beep warning, I don't change lane)
- The 360 camera is clear enough to use; any clearer and I think it's unnecessary because its main function is for reversing; a driver who's been driving for years and still needs a camera to parallel park is too poor at driving
- Electric parking brake automatic, convenient. I only turn on Auto Hold when the red light is long; otherwise I don't, because it's a bit sensitive, when creeping in traffic at an intersection it can activate.
- Automatic high beam with cornering lights: great if you get a chance to go far.
- Large infotainment screen, sufficient to use, no need to upgrade; for listening to music just plug in CarPlay (a wireless adapter costs 300k), if you need to watch YouTube then buy an Android box. Saves quite a bit.
- The horn is typical Toyota, sounds like a motorcycle => I bought a Denso horn to replace it, 300k self-install self-remove.
- Fog lights are a bit dim, not very helpful for night driving on dark roads => bought LED bulbs 300k and replaced them myself; no need for projector retrofit because I almost never drive at night on unlit roads (for those in the countryside, might consider projector retrofit, 1-3 million for good use).
- Keyless entry but opening with key blade, adequate to use. The trunk light is dim because it uses filament, bought T10 LED bulbs to replace, bright white, a few thousand dong. The reading lights are already LED so no need to change.
- Ambient interior lights are already there; if you want to upgrade, the power source is there, just tap into it, no need to buy a new set or cut wires.
- Sound: stock speakers sound flat, bought a speaker set for 2 million and self-installed self-removed, no need for amp or under-seat subwoofer, just enough for my needs.
- Window tint: not needed, dealerships just rip you off; because I only drive in the city and rarely at midday, I bought magnetic sunshades for 200k that cover the car completely, cool and comfortable. If I drive at midday, the driver's seat might be a bit hot because the windshield is not tinted, but it's fine, rarely happens.
- Roof noise from rain: it's true, bought sound deadening and applied myself for about 1 million; if you don't know how, a garage costs about 3 million, done.
- Engine noise: low because RPM is only 1000-1200, just turn up the music and it cancels out.
- Tire noise: yes, clear, especially on rough roads above 50 km/h, but in city driving you rarely reach that speed, and even if you do, there are no rough roads => not much of an issue, just turn up music to cancel.
- Wind noise: yes, a whoosh sound above 50 km/h => again rarely, not very important.
- Car body roll: it rolls like crazy, probably need to upgrade to Tein suspension to eliminate roll; don't listen to garage advice to install this or that strut bar, waste of money.
- Customer service: Toyota does very well, friendly, attentive; I haven't encountered the "beer with a treat" thing; as for being pushed to buy accessories, it depends on whether the customer is easy or not; from a business perspective, everyone wants to sell more.
- Aftermarket mods: many things available on Shopee, buy and install, if bored then remove; decals or interior wraps, wheel stickers, lots; buy to play, when bored take off and put something else. Even things that require removal like side steps, mudguards, etc., you can buy and install yourself.
- Maintenance: I buy cabin filter and replace myself, buy windshield washer fluid and refill myself; go to the dealership only for engine oil filter, fuel filter, etc. – minor stuff.
- Undercoating: don't listen to the dealership or garage, it doesn't rust or corrode anywhere. Those living near the coast or frequently driving through water should consider.
- Tires: just run them for maybe 50,000 km before changing; so when buying, it's best to immediately sell the Bridgestone tires as soon as you leave the dealership and install Primacy 5 tires, only a few million extra.

After buying the car, I only spent a few million more and still satisfied my passion for modding and DIY; more importantly, the car meets my needs practically, with low maintenance and servicing costs. As for fuel consumption, don't believe the manufacturer's claims regardless of brand; it depends on the specific traffic conditions of each place; driving non-stop for 20 minutes is different from driving 20 minutes with 15 minutes of stop-and-go traffic.

category: Value Perception; Value Perception; Brand Perception
parameter: Market Position; Model Refresh; Brand Reputation
content_english:
Before this, the competition between Toyota and Mitsubishi went like this:

Zace vs. Jolie (Jolie was slightly more popular). Then Toyota launched the Innova, and the Jolie faded into history.

Lancer vs. Camry, Corolla (at first, they competed on equal footing). Later, Toyota upgraded the Camry and Corolla and released the Vios, and the Lancer also faded away.

Pajero vs. Land Cruiser (initially, the Pajero seemed to outdo the Land Cruiser), but later Toyota made upgrades, and the Land Cruiser became a symbol for successful people, while the Pajero dropped to the budget segment.

Grandis vs. Previa (at first, the Grandis was a benchmark for automatic cars. When Toyota released the Previa, Grandis sales plummeted and production was discontinued. Meanwhile, the Previa went on to have several more improved versions.)

Outlander vs. Innova (the Outlander never made it onto the best-selling list like the Innova, which dominated for a long time).

Expander vs. Rush, Avanza (the Expander claimed the top spot), but when Toyota improved and launched the new Veloz and Avanza, who knows if the Expander's fate will be similar to its Mitsubishi predecessors?

content_english: Looks great in person, not as ugly as in the pictures. It's tidy and really cool, especially the white color. Most of the people who criticize it just can't afford it, and those who can afford it say it's not classy enough. For me, seeing it in person, it's very eye-catching, uniquely beautiful — not the angular aesthetic that many people want. To me, a car is a whole fortune. Whether it's an asset or a liability doesn't matter; what matters is what purpose it serves — for the family, enjoying trips, going to work or running errands when needed. Practical, spacious, strong 115hp engine, and plenty of safety features are my top criteria, and that's enough for me. Between the Xpander and the Veloz, I'd choose the Stargazer. I'll work hard for another year to bring it home. I'd rate it an 8, the Xpander a 7, and the Veloz a 6.

It depreciates quickly due to low demand. Vietnamese people, before buying a car, already calculate how much they can sell it for later, so this car loses out. This car is for middle-aged people who buy it for the family and don't want to fuss too much about money. It's very refined. If compared to a Korean car with a 2.0L engine, this one saves more fuel. category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance

content_english:
Issue with wind noise when driving on the highway at 80-90 km/h: I confirm there is sound coming from the front two wheels, but it's not as loud as the Veloz. If you play music at a moderate volume, it will mask the wind noise quite a bit. For this aspect, I guarantee that compared to the Veloz scoring 5 points, this car scores 7.5 points.

Swaying: if only the driver is driving, there will be a slight wobble. But if carrying 4 to 5 adults, it definitely does not sway like the Suzuki XL.

CVT when climbing steep slopes: I just went to Da Lat around October 20. Climbing the pass is no problem, my friend. When going up Chuoi Pass, Bao Loc, or Pren, I just put it in M2, and it climbs comfortably. For overtaking on the pass, I combine acceleration and shift to M3 or M4 to easily and smoothly overtake other cars.

CVT when descending steep slopes: I combine using M1 or M2 and the brake pedal depending on the steepness. It's still very smooth and uses little braking.

In summary: this car handles mountain passes with a load of 4 to 5 adults smoothly and quietly, my friend.

content_english: Car manufacturing in Vietnam, besides factors of price and appearance, must pay attention to the issue of fuel economy. Only the wealthy class can play with cars without considering fuel costs. The wealthy young generation prefers cars that accelerate instantly. But how many rich young people are there? The common people make up the majority. Ford caters to everyone. Those who need a pickup on a budget have the XL, XLT 2.2L. Those better off have the Wildtrak with the biturbo 2.0L. The rich will have the powerful Raptor that drinks fuel freely. I see that Nissan has a 1.9L diesel single-cab with enough power for highway driving. So for a 4x4, a 2.2L engine is enough, why use an engine over 2.5L? If it's already 4x4 and has a differential lock, is there any terrain it can't crawl through? Before 1975, my family had a Jeep Willys 4x4 without a differential lock, with 30 horsepower, yet it could crawl through any mountain or muddy terrain. The only thing it lacked compared to modern cars was speed due to low power. Now cars have nearly 200 horsepower plus a differential lock, so why install an engine that wastes fuel? Putting in a wasteful engine is for those with money to play with cars.

category: Powertrain / Performance; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Safety & ADAS; Styling / Design; Value Perception
parameter: Steering Feel; Lane Keep Assist; Visibility; Maneuverability; NVH; Air Conditioning; Headlight Design; Cabin Space; Trunk Space; ADAS Features; Exterior Design; Value for Money
content_english:
I just picked up the high-end full option version this afternoon on April 27th (I hadn't driven it even once before, just watched reviews on various channels, went to the showroom to see it in person, and decided to put down the money and arrange to bring it home today). Here's my assessment after driving it for a solid 3 hours:

- Steering: Light, very solid and stable feel. The lane-keeping assist is very useful, it will help new drivers a lot because it sticks to the lane flawlessly without complaint.
- Visibility: This is one point that might catch new drivers off guard. When I first got behind the wheel, visibility was somewhat obstructed at the A-pillar and rear quarter. But after 3 hours, my observation gradually adjusted, so this might be annoying at first but over time the driver will fully adapt.
- Car size in urban driving: Even though it's a 7-seat MPV, the sleek bullet-like design makes it feel very nimble in the city. Maneuvering with the side mirrors is very easy (even a newbie like me adapted quickly), and importantly, turning around is extremely fast and tight (if you're skilled, just 2-3 back-and-forth moves will do it quickly even on narrow busy streets).
- Noise: Not too loud, but not completely soundproof either. I haven't tried playing music yet, but if you do, the music should drown out any road noise.
- Automatic climate control: After pre-cooling from a distance, when you open the door and step in, the air inside is already cool. Cooling speed is quite satisfactory.
- Lights: Since I drove mainly around the city in the afternoon, the headlights are bright enough. The auto mode is very handy.
- Space: This is an absolute 10/10. I was really surprised by how spacious it is, especially the rear seats. My two kids (ages 6 and 4) can lie down fully stretched in the back row comfortably. As for the driver's seat and front passenger seat, they're incredibly roomy, no complaints.
- Cargo: If you fold the third-row seats, you can carry anything because it's so spacious.
- ADAS: This is a second 10/10 because the smart sense features from the manufacturer are incredibly helpful. The warnings are very sensitive when vehicles suddenly appear or get too close in front or behind.
- Finally, exterior design: Personally, I don't think it's as ugly as some others have reviewed. But I won't argue that the front end isn't eye-catching because it's indeed not as beautiful as designs from other brands. In short, overall exterior of this car, in my opinion, is acceptable.
- Long-distance driving feel: On April 30th, I'll drive back to my hometown for the holiday, so I don't have an opinion on this yet.

* Summary: 8 out of 10.
***Sincere advice: Anyone looking for a family car with a budget around 650 million VND, you should check out the mid-range version of this car. If you can stretch a little more, go for the high-end version. I believe if you choose this car, it won't disappoint you for the money you invest in a family vehicle.

Thanks everyone for reading my comment. If you have any questions or want to discuss, feel free to ask me—I'll answer.

category: Brand Perception; Brand Perception; Value Perception; Other; Brand Perception
parameter: Brand Strategy; Brand Trust; Maintenance Cost; Customization; Competitor Comparison
content_english:
The Nissan group and Suzuki have very poor business strategies. Recently I saw Suzuki only made minimal additions of electronic convenience equipment, but their advertising is weak. Learn from Ford. Ford's weakness is that the price of spare parts for repairs is too high. If they adjust the price of spare parts, Ford will be king of the world. Hyundai and Kia have no chance. Toyota relies on being a brand that entered Vietnam early. This brand is deeply ingrained in Vietnamese people, so it can still sell. Toyota is becoming more and more arrogant. Hyundai and Kia rely on electronic features that surpass Toyota. Toyota, on the other hand, cuts back features, only giving them to large-segment cars. Simple, cheap electronic features—why cut them? The central CPU is expensive only because of the chip and software license, but those random bits and pieces are not worth much. Ford has enough versions in each segment for every target audience.

The common weakness of all brands is that the design does not account for customers adding features according to their preferences. For example, if a customer wants to add a stop-start button, the company doesn't make it. If someone adds it themselves, the company won't warranty the electrical system. Taking it to outside shops, I dread the electricians. Most outside electricians do shoddy work, not up to standard, easily causing wire fires. Non-brand parts can easily cause the car to catch fire.

Each segment only needs two versions: manual and automatic. Customers who buy manual cars—whatever they want to add, we can accommodate. Safe for the car and satisfying for the buyer. With reasonable prices, no one would nitpick. Because when you add features, you get ripped off too much, so they give up.

content_english:
Comfort is above the XL7, but still below the Xpander in my opinion.

IVT is more fuel-efficient on the highway—RPM stays lower at high speeds.
AT is more responsive, but on the highway it's not as efficient as IVT.

Compared to the Reborn, the Reborn is definitely tougher, and the Reborn has bigger dimensions.

As for the Zenix, it also wins in terms of size and power. If you have the budget, go for the hybrid—its fuel consumption is definitely more efficient.

For the Destinator, it has big power and the suspension is softer than the Stargazer. Cabin insulation and vibration damping are also better.

But among those three cars, the Stargazer feels the most advanced in features: smart cruise control, Bose audio, cooled seats, captain seats, and the ambient lighting makes it feel like riding in a premium car. So it just depends on your needs.

category: Other; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Powertrain / Performance; Other; Safety & ADAS; Value Perception; Value Perception; Value Perception
parameter: Practicality; Interior & Exterior Design; Cabin Space; Seat Flexibility; Engine Power; Energy Consumption; Transmission; Driving Enjoyment; Transmission Option; Reliability; Airbag; Maintenance Cost; Tire Cost; Price
content_english: I have experienced driving 3 generations of X-Trail: T30, T31, and T32 (the current model). Basically, the X-Trail is a practical and durable model. Compared to the previous two generations T30 and T31, the T32 has been improved with much better interior and exterior, although the interior feels tighter because the seats are made flatter and the door details are also made thicker. In the T31, I could still put a large refrigerator in the car, but the T32 can't because the second-row seats don't fold flat. As for the engine, Nissan kept the same 2.5L engine across all three generations, powerful but thirsty. The Xtronic CVT has fixed the issues of the old CVT. My T30 had to be recalled for a policy repair, though it's been driven 140,000 km and still okay. The manual T31 has run 160,000 km and still runs great. My T31 manual combined with the 2.5L engine is truly a match made in heaven; with both axles 4x4, off-roading is also comfortable, very reasonable fuel consumption of 10L/100km combined. The T32 only has automatic transmission, so it loses that excitement. Currently, my T32 is the SV 2.5 4x4 version, still running well, only recently had a recall for the airbag. City driving gives 14L, combined 10-12L/100km depending on the road. No other costs incurred because nothing has broken. However, the Grandtrek tires on the car are quite expensive, over 5 million VND each. In Vietnam, that's the situation: the car is more fuel-thirsty than competitors in the same segment, maintenance costs are high if anything breaks or needs repair, and the price is too high, 100-200 million VND above its actual value. For a practical car, don't price it like a premium car.

category: New Tech; Styling / Design; Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Value Perception
parameter: 360 Camera Quality; Rearview Camera Obstruction; Cabin Space; Ride Stability; Acceleration at Low Battery; Fuel Consumption
content_english:
Minor drawbacks, but if upgraded it would be even better:
1) The 360-degree camera is low-res, a bit blurry, but still usable;
2) The camera on the windshield is a bit big; when turning at night, looking diagonally, the light gets blocked, quite obstructive, not as spacious and comfortable as the Vios.
The most worthwhile upgrade later would be a multi-link rear suspension—that would be better. When driving alone over uneven roads, you clearly feel the car swaying side to side constantly; in the Vios, this feeling is noticeably less.
Riding in the CX-8 or Carnival feels more solid, much more stable, without that constant rocking like this car.
Driving this car in Saigon, in stop-and-go traffic inch by inch, it gets about 7 L/100km; crawling at 20-30 km/h gives 6.6-6.7.
The biggest limitation is probably when stopped at a red light, for example. When you start moving and the battery is dead, the gas engine has to kick in. At that point, the engine has to both drive the generator to charge the battery and move the car from 0, so the engine makes a loud revving noise, yet the acceleration feels frustratingly weak and gutless, only feeling adequate once you’re above 30 km/h, with quicker response. This situation is probably more noticeable when climbing hills or mountain passes, especially when speed drops below 20 or 10 km/h, as the engine alone has to do double duty, leaving the car feeling sluggish and out of breath.

1. ?? ?n khoa
category: Comfort & Space; Comfort & Space; Powertrain / Performance; Styling / Design; Styling / Design; Safety & ADAS; Comfort & Space; Other; Safety & ADAS; Other; Other; Comfort & Space
parameter: NVH; NVH; Steering Feel; Interior Design; Dashboard Design; Safety Features; NVH; Engine Bay Layout; Body Structure; Build Quality; Spare Tire; Air Conditioning
content_english:
I have both cars now, so let me give an honest, personal review. I'm just a neutral observer—no brand loyalty at all.

1. Engine bay noise entering the cabin: Veloz is quieter. When driving, the Veloz absorbs bumps mainly from the 4 wheel wells. Both cars are similar to the VL10—the Xpander is also a 9 out of 10, not much difference.

2. The Veloz engine is quieter than the Xpander. When idling with the AC on and windows up, the Veloz is extremely quiet and smooth, while the Xpander has some noise entering the cabin and the car vibrates slightly. The Veloz doesn't.

3. While driving, the Xpander's steering feels light, so it wobbles a bit when turning, and the steering return after corners isn't complete—you have to push it a little more to straighten out. The Veloz steering is heavier, returns to center properly, and the self-centering is quite good. The D-CVT of the Veloz runs very smoothly, while the Xpander's 4-speed automatic jerks and the RPM hangs—when shifting, the RPM climbs to 3000 and stays there for 2-3 seconds before dropping.

4. Overall interior: Xpander looks better than Veloz, except for the dashboard. The Veloz has a fully digital cluster with no physical dials—it looks more premium.

5. Safety features and technology: Neither the Xpander nor even the Xpander Cross can compare to the Veloz—from number of airbags to brakes to active safety.

6. Third-row seating while moving: The Xpander has a droning noise, like being on an airplane—quite uncomfortable. The Veloz doesn't.

7. Engine bay of Xpander looks tidier and more organized than the Veloz.

8. Chassis platform: Veloz on DNGA, Xpander on RISE. If you look closely, DNGA is actually better quality because it uses more high-strength steel in the frame than Xpander's RISE.

content_english:
Based on my test drive and sitting in both cars. *DRIVING FEEL – Here I want to make it clear that the Expander feels solid when driving, the car doesn't feel like it's wandering or floating at all, the car goes where you point it. At high speeds the car tracks well, no need for constant steering corrections. *BUILD QUALITY – Here I mean chassis quality and body panels. The Veloz engine bay feels flimsy, opening the hood I already gave it a minus point. The engine is poorly insulated. Also the Veloz's sheet metal is thin (the manufacturer explains thin metal to reduce fuel consumption and make the car lighter). *CABIN SPACE – The Veloz's front row is very narrow and feels cramped on both sides when sitting. The Expander's width feels roomier and more comfortable when sitting (I even brought a tape measure to measure precisely and the interior space in the Expander > Veloz). I traveled with 6 people, a full load of 5 suitcases (1 large, 4 medium), 2 backpacks, and the Veloz couldn't fit them all (couldn't stuff them in and they stuck out). Meanwhile in the Expander, I folded one side of the third row and stacked medium suitcases up to the roof, with 2 young men sharing the remaining seat, and drove from Hue to Dong Ha. I even put a medium suitcase on the center armrest. With the same amount of luggage and people, there was no way to avoid having suitcases on laps when my family took the Veloz. *INTERIOR QUALITY – Basically, when you get into both cars you can see which one has more and better leather. The Expander's leather trim design > Veloz. The seats feel more premium, and even small details like armrests and the dashboard are leather-wrapped. 
=> That's enough. In terms of day-to-day practicality, I rate the Expander as having more of what I need compared to the three criteria in the review (choosing based on fuel cost, safety systems – the noise difference of 3-4 dB is something the ear can barely tell at this segment, once you turn up the music a bit they're the same). Compared to those criteria, the Expander is completely fine and acceptable. thanks

Thank you, sir, for watching and supporting my channel. From what I understand, let me share with you the following:

1. When you start the car, the initial mode will be fully electric (except when the battery is very low, then the gasoline engine will start to charge; otherwise, it will be electric). At that time, devices like lights, horn, and screen draw power from that small 12V battery, similar to when you turn off the car but still honk the horn or turn on hazard lights—they draw power from that. It’s also possible the hybrid battery supplies a little (I’m not sure about this). And when the car detects low or near-depleted charge, the hybrid battery (and gasoline engine) will activate to charge immediately, sir.

2. When you turn on the air conditioner, if the battery has plenty of charge, I notice the car uses the battery—just like when you start moving, the car runs purely on electric, right? The air conditioning still blows cold, doesn’t it? But because the hybrid battery in this car is small, it can only run fully electric for about 1-2 km at most, then the gasoline engine immediately kicks in to both charge the battery and drive the car.

That’s my understanding and knowledge on this, sir. If anything is unclear, feel free to ask.

content_english:
Bro, a lithium-ion battery that many claim lasts 10 years without degradation — that's really hard to believe.

Cheap hybrid cars use an electric motor and charge/discharge continuously; after just 3-5 years, the battery cell capacity drops to 50-60%.

parameter: NVH; Engine Smoothness; Ride Quality; Cabin Space; Ownership Experience
content_english:
Thank you to Mr. Việt Anh and Tipcar. I’m 27, and this is my first car. When I chose this car, my family, coworkers, and friends were all surprised. Some said it’s an old person’s car, criticized it for being plastic, thin sheet metal, weak engine... I still remember the day I went to get the license plates; some other car owners tapped on my car and made comments, but I still kept quiet. Back then, Tipcar didn’t have a video on this model, only the hybrid version, so I didn’t have much info on the experience from Tipcar. I watched other channels for reference but didn’t think much of it!

I’ve been driving it for almost 4 months, coming up on the 5,000 km service. My mom gets carsick very easily, but in this car she’s comfortable and doesn’t feel sick. The sound insulation is quite good, the engine is smooth, gentle, and suits my driving style. The space is roomy and fits a family of six (I plan to install a roof rack soon). I’m happy with my choice because this is a car I trust and it brings value to my family. Many people may have their own opinions, comparing it to other models or even the previous rear-wheel-drive Innova, but to me a good car is one you’re satisfied with and the people riding with you are satisfied too. My family, friends, and coworkers all give positive feedback about the car, so that’s enough for me! Once again, thank you Tipcar for making a video about the car I own—my first—right at this Tet holiday.

content_original: bác nào thích d?ch chuy?n d?n qua xu h??ng g?m cao cao chút ?? r?nh k?t h?p camping thì con xe nào h?p s? CVT thì lo?i n
content_english: If you like to go to high ground clearance areas and combine it with camping when you have free time, then for any car with a CVT transmission, eliminate it right from the start when choosing to buy. This transmission is only for driving around in the city, not suitable for carrying full loads and climbing steep slopes because it reduces the lifespan of the belt. Moreover, at around 40,000 km you must change the transmission oil, and when reaching 100,000 km you must replace the belt. If you want genuine service, you have to replace the entire transmission costing nearly 200 million VND. If you go to a private garage, it's a few tens of million but not genuine, and you lose warranty if the transmission fails and the belt is replaced. Car manufacturers know well the pros and cons of CVT transmissions, but they still use them because it reduces the cost of the car. They do not clearly state the pros and cons in the manual about the belt. If any of you don't believe, then watch the YouTube clips of cars that have to go to private garages to replace the belt.

content_english: Just went on a family road trip to Quy Nhon, Da Nang, Hoi An, Nha Trang, using an Innova Cross and a 2023 Carnival. Summary: sitting in the third row of the Carnival was incredibly uncomfortable because the car swayed badly on rough national highways, and on expressways it bounced so much at high speed that people were thrown up — very unpleasant. When we got out, nobody could eat; we just lay down and only felt fine the next day. The Innova Cross was the complete opposite — everyone was happy, went out, ate and drank well right after getting off. The two cars drove side by side the whole trip. The Carnival had a lot of luggage on the roof rack; the Innova had normal luggage. Women and children commented: riding in the Carnival felt like torture — neck strain, headache, had to take painkillers. When buying a car, the passenger experience is really important. Long-term use of a car that causes discomfort and hassle is no good for the owner. Hope you all find a car you're happy with!

content_english:
Thank you to Anh Viet Anh and Tipcar. I'm 27 years old, and this is my first car. When I chose this car, my family, colleagues, and friends were also surprised. Some people said it was an old person's car, criticized it as being made of plastic, thin sheet metal, weak engine... I still remember the day I went to get the license plate, some other car owners tapped on my car and criticized it, but I still ignored them. At that time, Tipcar didn't have a video about this model, only the Hybrid version, so I didn't have much information about the experience with this car from Tipcar. I just watched other channels for reference and didn't think much of it!

I've been driving the car for almost 4 months, and the 5,000 km scheduled maintenance is coming up. My mom gets carsick very easily, but riding in this car, she feels comfortable and doesn't get sick. The sound insulation is quite good, the engine is smooth and gentle, and it suits my driving style. The space is spacious and fits a family of 6 (I plan to install a roof rack soon). I'm happy with my choice, because this is a car I trust and it brings value to my family. Many people may have their own opinions, compare it to other models or even the previous rear-wheel-drive Innova, but a good car for me is one that I'm satisfied with and that those who ride with me are also satisfied with. And my family, friends, and colleagues all give positive feedback about the car, so that's enough for me! Once again, thank you Tipcar for making a video about the first car I own, right during this festive season.

The Fortuner is okay for off-road driving, but on city streets it's worse than a stone being kicked. Fuel consumption is high. The Fortuner is the most truck-like vehicle in Toyota's lineup. Since Toyota launched the Corolla Cross, the driving is much smoother because Toyota has made many improvements to the steering system—it doesn't drive with a harsh rumble like the Altis 2017-2018. This Innova Cross is an excellent choice for families.

parameter: Ride Quality; Cabin Space; Engine Power; Fuel Economy; Handling; Steering Feel; Competitor Comparison; Family Suitability
content_english: Fortuner runs like a truck—extremely boring to drive, sluggish, doesn't grip the road in corners, the gasoline engine is unresponsive and stiff, and the 4WD is also dull. The Fortuner is okay for off-road, but on city streets it's as boring as a stone cow. High fuel consumption. The Innova is completely the opposite: light, smooth, spacious, the 2.0L engine is adequate, the improved driving feel is better, cornering steering is responsive—unlike the joint-venture Altis, Vios, and Camry 2013-2018. The Fortuner is the most truck-like vehicle in Toyota's lineup. Since Toyota released the Corolla Cross, the driving is much smoother because Toyota has greatly improved the steering system—it doesn't feel jerky like the 2017-2018 Altis. This Innova Cross is an excellent choice for families.

content_english: I don't think at this price it can beat the Xpander AT Premium 2025. Actually, there's no way it can beat any of them, unless you're forced to use an electric car. Even comparing electric and gas is just too mismatched. But if we're still going to make that comparison, the Cam360 has no camera, no sensors, no cruise control. The exterior looks better, but what good is looks when the ride quality is more important? You'll be worrying about every bump and scratch. The interior is unacceptable :))). Seriously, I can't accept it. Compared to the Xpander's interior, it's a disgrace. Now let's compare engines. The car's got a crappy 1.5L that's enough for you to overtake, handle mountain roads, even when fully loaded. But more importantly, even if it's weak, it's very durable :)). Tough as nails and fuel efficient. Cheap maintenance. As for the chassis, Mitsubishi has the Rise frame that absorbs impacts like hitting an elephant—acceptable, stiff and bouncy, but more durable than others. As for the Limo, I haven't tested it yet, so I won't say, but I bet it'll fall apart :)). Yeah, one costs 750 million, the other 760 million on the road. What do you guys think? parameter: Brand Strategy; Infrastructure Readiness; Consumer Readiness; Brand Image; Export Strategy; Financial Sustainability; Strategic Direction
content_english: Vinfast's mistake was abandoning fossil fuel engines. Not every country can fully invest in electricity and energy infrastructure, especially when coupled with consumer habits that cannot yet change. Being too hasty and trying to get ahead of the curve has made Vinfast a guinea pig. If Vinfast had continued developing fossil fuel vehicles, it could have exported to any country and set up service dealerships there, building a solid foundation for Vinfast in those markets. The cash flow would have been healthier to continue investing in emerging areas like electric vehicles, and if EVs failed, it wouldn't have been a big deal. For now, stop developing EVs before it's too late.
